content:
6 min read
3 hours ago

--

As someone working in DevSecOps and API Security, I spend a lot of time understanding how web applications and APIs communicate. Recently, while going through the latest RFCs published by the IETF, I came across RFC 10008, which introduces a brand-new HTTP method called QUERY.
My first reaction was probably the same as most developers:
“Wait… don’t we already have POST? Why do we need another HTTP method?”
I assumed this would be another small protocol update that wouldn’t have much practical impact.
But after spending some time reading the RFC and understanding the reasoning behind it, I realized that this change isn’t about adding new functionality. Instead, it’s about making HTTP communicate intent much more clearly.
If you’re hearing about QUERY for the first time, this blog should help you understand why it exists and whether it actually matters.
A Quick Refresher on HTTP Methods
Before talking about QUERY, let's quickly revisit the HTTP methods that we use almost every day.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Most developers, including me at one point, associate POST with creating resources. While that’s a very common use case, POST actually has a much broader meaning.
According to HTTP semantics, POST simply means “Here is some data. Process it.”
Notice that it doesn’t specifically say “create a resource.”
A POST request can:

• Create a user

• Upload a file

• Send an email

• Authenticate a user

• Generate a report

• Execute a search
All of these are perfectly valid uses of POST.
The Problem with GET
Imagine you’re building an employee management portal.
Initially, searching for employees is straightforward.
GET /employees?department=SecuritySimple, readable and easy to understand.
But modern applications rarely perform simple searches anymore.
GET /employees?department=Security&country=India& city=Bangalore&experience=5&employmentType=FullTime&salaryMin=100000&salaryMax=250000&project=Mythos&skills=Python&skills=GCP&skills=Kubernetes&certification=CISSP&certification=OSCP&sortBy=experience&sortOrder=desc&joinedAfter=2025–01–01&joinedBefore=2026–01–01&page=1&pageSize=100Very quickly the URL becomes extremely large. Instead of one or two query parameters, we now have dozens.
Besides becoming difficult to read, long URLs introduce practical challenges.

• Some browsers and infrastructure components have URL length limits.

• Query strings become difficult to maintain.

• Nested JSON structures don’t naturally fit into URLs.

• Query parameters frequently appear in browser history and various server or proxy logs.
Clearly, GET starts becoming uncomfortable for complex searches.
Does POST Solve This Problem?
Actually… Yes, it does.
This is exactly why many APIs today expose endpoints like:
POST /employees/search{
"department": "Security",
"experience": 5,
"skills": [
"Python",
"GCP",
"Kubernetes"
]
}From a developer’s perspective, this is fantastic.
The request body can contain:

• Large payloads

• Complex JSON

• Arrays

• Nested objects

• Rich search filters
Everything works exactly as expected. So naturally my next question was…
If POST already solves the problem, why introduce QUERY?
The Real Issue Was Never Technical
This is where I had my biggest misconception. I initially thought RFC 10008 was trying to fix a limitation in POST. It isn’t. POST has never had a problem carrying search requests. The real issue is something called HTTP semantics.
That simply means that the HTTP method should accurately describe what the request is intended to do.
Let’s look at four different requests.
Creates a new user — POST /users
Authenticates a user — POST /login
Generates a report — POST /generate-report
Searches employees — POST /employees/search
Notice something interesting?
I know this is intresting but from your application’s perspective, these four endpoints do completely different things.
But from HTTP’s perspective they’re all simply POST
HTTP itself has no idea whether your POST request is:

• Creating something

• Updating something

• Deleting something

• Simply searching
Only your application knows.
Enter QUERY
RFC 10008 introduces a new HTTP method called QUERY.
Now, instead of writing:
POST /employees/searchYou can write:
QUERY /employees{
"department": "Security",
"experience": 5,
"skills": [
"Python",
"GCP",
"Kubernetes"
]
}If we notice, The JSON body or the database or the response query didn’t change. Only one word changed POST became QUERY.
And surprisingly, that one word makes a huge difference in how the request is described.
Understanding “Safe” Doesn’t Mean “Secure”
One term you’ll frequently see while reading RFC 10008 is that QUERY is a Safe HTTP Method.
The word “safe” confused me initially because it sounds like it has something to do with security. It doesn’t.
Within HTTP, safe has a very specific definition. A safe request means:
Executing the request should not change the application’s state on the server.
That means no:

• Creating new records

• Updating existing records

• Deleting data

• Changing configurations

• Triggering business actions that permanently modify information
The request simply retrieves information.
One thing worth noting is that the server will still consume CPU, memory, and database resources while processing a QUERY request. That’s completely normal.
Those temporary resource usages are not considered a change in server state.
When HTTP says “safe,” it is referring to persistent application state, not temporary system resource usage.
Again So Why Is This Important?
When we build APIs, we often think only about our application. But every request usually travels through multiple systems before reaching our backend.
Browser -> CDN -> Load Balancer -> Reverse Proxy -> API Gateway -> Web Server -> Application
Most of these components do not understand your application’s business logic. They only understand HTTP.
When they see POST , They only know one thing that this request might modify server state.
When they see QUERY they immediately know something else this request is intended only to retrieve information.
That’s the value RFC 10008 brings. It improves communication between every HTTP-aware component without changing the application’s functionality.
What Does This Mean for Security Engineers?
From a security perspective, this RFC doesn’t suddenly change how we perform API security testing.

• Broken Access Control is still Broken Access Control.

• SQL Injection is still SQL Injection.

• Business Logic vulnerabilities are still Business Logic vulnerabilities.
However, there are a few things that security teams should keep in mind.
As the industry gradually adopts QUERY, security tools will need to recognize a new HTTP method.
Web Application Firewalls (WAFs), API Gateways, reverse proxies, vulnerability scanners, custom scripts, and penetration testing tools will eventually need to support QUERY alongside GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE.
Security engineers should also remember that safe does not mean public.
Just because a QUERY request doesn’t modify data doesn’t mean everyone should be allowed to execute it.
Authentication and authorization remain just as important as they are for GET requests.
Will Every Website Start Using QUERY Tomorrow?
Definitely not. One thing I’ve learned about internet standards is that adoption takes time.

• Browsers need to support it.

• HTTP libraries need to support it.

• Frameworks need to support it.

• API Gateways need to support it.

• Reverse proxies need to support it.

• Cloud providers need to support it.
Only after the surrounding ecosystem matures will developers begin adopting QUERY more widely.
Even then, many organizations may decide not to migrate existing APIs because their current POST-based search endpoints already work perfectly well.
I expect we’ll first see QUERY appear in newly designed APIs rather than existing ones being rewritten.
My Final Thoughts
Press enter or click to view image in full size

When I first saw RFC 10008, I genuinely wondered why the IETF was introducing another HTTP method when POST already seemed capable of handling everything.
After understanding the reasoning behind it, my perspective changed.
QUERY isn't trying to replace POST.

• It isn’t making APIs faster.

• It isn’t reducing server load.

• It isn’t introducing a revolutionary new capability.
Instead, it gives developers a more accurate way to express something they’ve been doing for years — performing complex, read-only operations using a request body.
Sometimes, good engineering isn’t about adding more features. Sometimes it’s simply about making communication clearer.
And in my opinion, that’s exactly what RFC 10008 achieves.
If you actually read this entire blog… congratulations! You have a longer attention span than most production deployments. 😄 Just kidding! Thanks for reading, and see you in the next blog. 👋
